macro_segments.title: The Debate over Water Libations and the Requirement of a Service Vessel

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the debate between Rabbi Eliezer and the Sages regarding three logs of water outside the Temple
The definition of the dispute: whether a kli sharet (service vessel) is required to sanctify water for libations
Examination of whether libations were performed on private altars when they were permitted, and the implications for liability outside the Temple

macro_segments.title: Wine Libations: The Parallel Case and the Heaping of Liquid

micro_segments.title: Teaching about one who pours three logs of wine outside: Rabbi Eliezer ben Rabbi Shimon requires sanctification in a vessel
Rav Adda bar Abba's explanation: the dispute concerns the heaping of the liquid (beirutzei midos) at the rim of the vessel
Rava's statement that libations were brought on private altars without a service vessel, establishing a precedent

macro_segments.title: Private Altars and the Requirement of Libations: Tannaitic Dispute

micro_segments.title: Teaching from the Braisa: the case of a private altar that does not require libations according to Rabbi
The Sages' position: libations are required even on private altars
Connection between the tannaitic debate and whether libations require a service vessel

macro_segments.title: Scriptural Interpretation: When Libations Became Obligatory—Rabbi Yishmael vs. Rabbi Akiva

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yishmael's reading of 'Ki tavo' (when you come): libations required only in a central location after entering the land
Rabbi Akiva's reading: 'moshvoseichem' (your dwellings) indicates libations were permitted on every private altar
The Gemara's inference: if Rabbi Akiva holds libations occurred on private altars, he believes they also occurred in the wilderness

macro_segments.title: The Sprinkling of Remaining Blood: Rabbi Nechemya's Position and Its Implications

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Nechemya's teaching: one who sprinkles remaining blood outside incurs liability
Rabbi Yochanan's interpretation: Rabbi Nechemya holds that sprinkling of shirayim is essential (meakev) for the atonement
Rabbi Akiva's objection: remaining blood is not central to the atonement process; comparison with offering of inner organs
Rav Ada bar Ahava's resolution: the debate applies only to inner sin offerings; by outer offerings, all agree sprinkling shirayim is not essential

macro_segments.title: Slaughter and Offering of Birds: The Interplay of Valid and Invalid Acts

micro_segments.title: Case: melikah (pinching) inside, then raising outside the Temple—one is liable
Case: melikah outside, then raising outside—one is exempt because melikah outside invalidates the offering
Case: shechitah (slaughter) inside, then raising outside—one is exempt because slaughter inside is invalid for birds
Case: shechitah outside, then raising outside—one is liable because slaughter outside is the valid act for birds
Principle: the manner of liability inside differs from the manner of liability outside; each act's validity depends on its location

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's General Principle and Its Application to Birds and Other Cases

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's teaching: whatever one is liable for outside, one is liable for the equivalent inside
Examination of whether Rabbi Shimon's principle applies to the melikah case
The Gemara determines that Rabbi Shimon's principle applies to shechitah of birds, with that case excepted from the general rule
Zeiri's and Rava's examples: slaughter at night and reception of blood in non-sacred vessels as additional cases supporting Rabbi Shimon's principle
Abuhah of Shmuel bar Rav Yitzchak's alternative reading: Rabbi Shimon reversed—if one is liable inside, one is liable outside

macro_segments.title: Blood of a Sin Offering Received in One or Two Vessels and the Conditions for Liability

micro_segments.title: Case: blood received in one vessel; whether sprinkled inside, outside, or partially in both locations, one is liable
Case: blood received in two vessels, both used inside—exempt, as only the first use effects atonement
Case: blood received in two vessels, both used outside—liable for both, as each is fit for the altar if needed
Case: blood received in two vessels, one inside and one outside—exempt, as using one inside invalidates the other
Case: blood received in two vessels, one outside and one inside—liable, as using one outside does not invalidate the other
Analogy to two sin offerings: when one animal is offered inside, the second becomes invalid, but offering one outside does not invalidate the other

topical_tags.term: Rauy L'Pesach Ohel Moed (Fit for the Tent Entrance)
